slides.title: Buod ng Unang Linggo
slides.text: Binabati kita! Natapos mo ang lahat ng slide sa Week 1. Natutunan natin ang kahulugan ng tiwala sa sarili ngayon. Natukoy natin ang pagkakaiba ng hilig at kakayahan natin. Nalaman natin ang kahalagahan ng pagsasanay at lakas-loob. Iniwasan natin ang pagyayabang at maling pag-iisip doon. Handa ka na ba sa pagsusulit at hamon ng linggong ito? Ipakita ang iyong tiwala sa sarili sa pagsagot ngayon! Ipagpatuloy ang pagiging disiplinadong mag-aaral ng GMRC!

examples.title: Tandaan
examples.content: Tiwala = Paniniwala sa sarili. Hilig = Gusto. Kakayahan = Galing.

standard.q: Ano ang tawag sa paniniwala sa sariling galing at kakayahan?
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang halimbawa ng 'hilig' o interes?
options: mahusay mag-drawing ng hayop
gustong magbasa ng mga aklat sa hapon
magaling magkuwenta ng barya
mabilis tumakbo sa kalsada

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang halimbawa ng 'kakayahan' o talento?
options: gustong manood ng sining
mahusay sumayaw ng Tinikling
gustong maglakad sa plaza
gustong makinig ng kanta

standard.q: Ano ang dapat mong gawin upang mapahusay ang iyong talento sa pagguhit?
options: itago ang mga krayola
magsanay gumuhit araw-araw
ihambing ang sarili sa iba
sumuko sa unang pagkakamali

standard.q: Paano mo maipapakita ang tiwala sa sarili sa loob ng silid-aralan?
options: matulog sa klase
itaas ang kamay upang sumagot sa tanong ng guro
makipag-away sa katabi
magtago sa ilalim ng desk

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang HINDI nagpapakita ng tiwala sa sarili?
options: sumubok sumagot kahit hindi sigurado
sumuko agad dahil natatakot magkamali
humingi ng tulong sa guro

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na asal kapag ikaw ay mas magaling sa matematika kaysa sa iyong kalaro?
options: turuan siya nang may kababaang-loob
magyabang sa buong klase

standard.q: Paano lumalago ang talento ng isang bata sa pag-awit?
options: sa pamamagitan ng panonood lamang
sa pamamagitan ng araw-araw na pagsasanay
sa pamamagitan ng pagbili ng bagong sapatos
sa pamamagitan ng pagtulog

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin kapag nagkamali ka sa pagguhit sa iyong klase?
options: itapon ang papel at umiyak
tanggapin ito bilang bahagi ng pagkatuto at sumubok muli
makipag-away sa katabi
sumuko na sa pagguhit

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang bawat bata ay may kani-kanilang sariling hilig at kakayahan.
Aling katangian ang ipinapakita ng batang hindi natatakot magsalita sa harap ng klase?
options: lakas ng loob at tiwala sa sarili

standard.q: Ano ang layunin ng pagbabahagi ng iyong talento sa iyong kapwa?
options: upang makakuha ng pera
upang maglingkod at magbigay-inspirasyon sa iba
upang maging sikat sa barangay

standard.q: Punan ang patlang: 'Ang pagsasanay at pagsisikap ay nagdudulot ng ___ ng kakayahan.'
options: pag-unlad at paghusay

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang pambalana sa tungkulin ng tao na nagtuturo ng mabuting asal?
Ano ang dapat iwasan kapag ikaw ay nagpapakita ng iyong talento sa pagtugtog?
options: pagngiti sa mambabasa
pagyayabang at pagmamaliit sa hindi marunong
pag-ensayo bago magsimula
paghingi ng gabay sa guro

standard.q: Saan natin maaaring gamitin ang ating kakayahan sa pagguhit sa barangay?
options: sa pagsira ng pader
sa paggawa ng mga poster para sa kalinisan sa barangay hall
sa pagtapon ng basura sa ilog
sa pagsusulat sa mga libro ng aklatan

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang paghahambing ng sarili sa iba ay nakatutulong upang tumibay ang tiwala sa sarili.
Aling ugali ang ipinapakita ng mag-aaral na nagpapasalamat sa puna ng kanyang guro?
options: kababaang-loob at kagustuhang matuto

standard.q: Aling salita ang naglalarawan ng kilos na may tiwala sa sarili?
options: pagsubok at pagsisikap

standard.q: Ano ang tinutukoy ng araling GMRC?
options: wastong pagkuwenta ng barya
mabuting asal at tamang pag-uugali (Character Education)
pagbasa ng mga mapa
pag-uri ng mga solid

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang tamang kaisipan kapag nahihirapan sa science?
options: 'Hindi ko talaga kaya ito.'
'Magsisikap ako at magtatanong sa aking guro upang matuto.'
'Hindi mahalaga ang science.'
'Ipagpapaliban ko na lang ang pag-aaral.'

standard.q: Tukuyin ang hilig sa pangungusap: 'Gusto ni Kiko na mag-alaga ng mga halaman sa hardin.'
options: mag-alaga ng mga halaman

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang tiwala sa sarili ay nangangahulugang alam mo na ang lahat at hindi mo na kailangang mag-aral.
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang magandang gawin kapag nakakaramdam ng takot sa harap ng klase?
options: tumakbo palabas ng silid-aralan
huminga nang malalim at isiping kaya mong magbahagi
umiyak sa harap ng guro

standard.q: Saan dapat magsimula ang pag-unlad ng iyong talento?
options: sa panonood ng iba lamang
sa iyong sariling puso, pagsasanay, at pagsisikap
sa pagbili ng mga laruan
sa pagtulog nang mahaba

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling kilos ang pinakamahusay na nagpapakita ng ugnayan ng tiwala sa sarili at pagtulong sa kapwa?
options: pagyayabang sa kalaro na ikaw ang pinakamabilis tumakbo
pagtuturo sa iyong kaklase na nahihirapan sa pagbasa nang may pasensya at tiwala
pagtatago ng iyong mga aklat upang hindi sila makabasa
pagsasabing hindi nila kayang matutunan ang iyong galing

challenge.q: Challenge: Paano mo mapagtatagumpayan ang takot na sumali sa street dancing sa pista ng inyong barangay?
options: magtago sa loob ng bahay tuwing pista
magsanay nang mabuti kasama ang grupo at magtiwala na kaya mong sumayaw sa harap ng tao
sabihing hindi maganda ang sayaw
pagtawanan ang mga sumasayaw sa parada

challenge.q: Challenge: Kung ang iyong katabi sa klase ay tahimik at natatakot magsalita, paano mo siya matutulungan gamit ang iyong tiwala?
options: sabihan siyang duwag siya sa harap ng iba
ganyakin siya nang malumanay at sabihing kaya rin niyang sumubok sumagot
huwag siyang pansinin dahil hindi mo naman siya kamag-anak
sumagot para sa kanya sa lahat ng pagkakataon

challenge.q: Challenge: Bakit ang pagsuko agad sa unang pagkakamali ay itinuturing na kawalan ng tiwala sa sarili?
options: Dahil naniniwala ka na hindi ka na matututo o mapapahusay ang iyong galing sa pamamagitan ng pagsasanay.
Dahil ang pagkakamali ay isang permanenteng kabiguan sa science.
Dahil mas madali ang matulog kaysa mag-ensayo.
Dahil ang guro ay nagagalit kapag may mali.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling katangian ang pinaka-kailangan ng isang Grade 3 student upang malinang ang kanyang natatanging talento sa sining?
options: pagbili ng pinakamahal na krayola
disiplina sa araw-araw na pagsasanay at bukas na isip sa paggabay ng guro
pagyayabang sa mga kaibigan
pag-iwas sa pagguhit ng mga bagay sa kalikasan

performance.title: Aking Kwento ng Galing
performance.desc: Sumulat ng isang talata o maikling kwento tungkol sa iyong paboritong hilig at kakayahan sa papel. Iguhit ang iyong sarili habang ginagawa ang talento na ito (hal. kumakanta, nag-aaral ng math, o nagtatanim). Ipakita ang iyong drawing at basahin ang iyong talata sa iyong magulang nang may tiwala at malinaw na tinig.
labels: Wastong natukoy at nailarawan ang sariling hilig at kakayahan
Maayos na naiguhit ang sarili habang ginagawa ang talento
Malinaw at may lakas ng loob na naibahagi ang ulat sa pamilya

pages:
Pagsasanay 1: Hilig vs. Kakayahan

Isulat kung ang sitwasyon ay nagpapakita ng Hilig (gusto) o Kakayahan (talento/galing) gamit ang stylus.

1. Mahusay sumayaw ng Tinikling si Caloy sa programa.
Kakayahan
2. Gustong-gustong manood ng mga bituin si Matteo sa gabi.
Hilig
3. Magaling magkuwenta ng matematika si Lani sa klase.
Kakayahan

Pagsasanay 2: Asal ng may Tiwala sa Sarili

Isulat kung ang ipinapakitang pag-uugali ay Mabuti (karapat-dapat) o Mali (dapat iwasan).

1. Pagtuturo sa kalaro ng pagbasa nang walang pagyayabang.
Mabuti
2. Pagsuko agad sa pagguhit dahil nabahiran ng dumi ang papel.
Mali
3. Pagtataas ng kamay upang sumubok sumagot sa tanong ng guro.
Mabuti

Pagsasanay 3: Paglutas sa Takot

Sagutin ang tanong gamit ang tumpak na detalye batay sa iyong sariling karanasan at aralin.

Ano ang gagawin mo kapag natatakot kang mag-report sa harap ng iyong mga kaklase?
Hihinga ako nang malalim, maghahanda nang mabuti, at magtitiwala na kaya kong magbahagi.
Iisipin ko na ang aking guro at kaklase ay naroon upang makinig at tumulong sa akin.

slides.title: Story: The Little Red Hen - Part 1
slides.text: Once upon a time, a hardworking and friendly little red hen lived on a sunny farm alongside three very lazy companions: a dog, a cat, and a duck. The lazy dog spent all his hours lying on the soft, warm wooden porch, sleeping in the hot sun and refusing to chase any sticks or play with the children. The sleepy cat loved to curl up in a cozy, cushioned chair in the farmer's kitchen, purring loudly and dreaming of drinking fresh milk all day. The noisy duck stayed in the cool pond all day, swimming in circles, quacking loudly, and playing in the mud without a care in the world. One bright morning, as the little red hen was busy scratching in the dark dirt looking for worms to feed her babies, she found some golden grains of wheat. She realized they could grow food. 'Who will help me plant this wheat?' she asked. 'Not I,' barked the lazy dog from the porch. 'Not I,' purred the sleepy cat from the chair. 'Not I,' quacked the noisy duck from the pond. 'Then I will plant it all by myself,' said the hen. And she did, working hard under the morning sun.

slides.title: Story: The Little Red Hen - Part 2
slides.text: The little red hen worked hard to plant the grains of wheat in the soil, digging small holes and watering them every morning. The warm sun shone down, and soon the green wheat grew tall, strong, and golden. 'Who will help me cut this tall wheat?' she asked the others. 'Not I,' barked the lazy dog from the porch. 'Not I,' purred the sleepy cat from the chair. 'Not I,' quacked the noisy duck from the pond. So the little red hen harvested the wheat all by herself, cutting each stem carefully. Next, she needed to grind the wheat into flour. 'Who will help me carry this heavy sack of wheat to the mill?' she asked. 'Not I,' said the dog. 'Not I,' said the cat. 'Not I,' said the duck. So she carried the heavy sack to the mill herself, watched the miller grind it into fresh white flour, and walked all the way back to the farm alone, carrying the heavy flour bag without complaining once.

slides.title: Story: The Little Red Hen - Part 3
slides.text: When she finally returned to the farmyard, she asked, 'Who will help me bake this white flour into bread?' 'Not I,' said the dog. 'Not I,' said the cat. 'Not I,' said the duck. So the little red hen mixed the dough and baked a warm, golden loaf of fresh bread. The delicious smell spread all over the farmyard. The lazy dog woke up and ran to the kitchen. The sleepy cat jumped down from the chair. The noisy duck waddled in from the pond. 'Who will help me eat this bread?' asked the hen. 'I will!' barked the dog. 'I will!' purred the cat. 'I will!' quacked the duck. 'No, you won't,' said the hen. 'None of you helped me plant the seed, cut the wheat, carry it to the mill, or bake the bread. I did all the work myself. Now, my little chicks and I will eat it ourselves.' And they did, eating every single crumb while the other animals watched hungrily, regretting their laziness.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Grains - Small, hard seeds of food plants like wheat.
• Harvested - Gathered mature crops.
• Lazy - Unwilling to work or use energy.

Phonics Focus: Practice the ending '-ed' sound in 'harvested' (id) and 'lived' (d).

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Which animals lived with the little red hen?
2. What did the other animals say when asked to help plant the wheat?
3. Why did the hen decide not to share her bread at the end?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Hard work and cooperation.

Those who do not participate in the work should not expect to share in the rewards. Cooperation makes tasks easier and builds a kind community.

Discuss: What is a chore you did recently to help your family?

slides.title: Poem: My Shadow - Part 1
slides.text:
I have a little shadow that goes in and out with me,
And what can be the use of him is more than I can see.
He is very, very like me from the heels up to the head;
And I see him jump before me, when I jump into my bed.

This curious dark shadow copies every single move I make. If I wave my left hand, he waves his hand on the ground. If I tilt my head to the side, he tilts his head too. Sometimes he seems to mimic my walks and runs. When the morning sun shines brightly behind me, he stretches long and wide across the green grass. He is my silent companion, walking beside me all day. He never speaks a single word, but he is always there, following my footsteps wherever I decide to go. He makes me feel like I am never alone, playing with me in the yard and running under the tall trees. This shadow appears because my body blocks the light from the sun or a lamp. Light travels in straight lines, and when it hits an object it cannot pass through, it leaves a dark area behind it. I love to watch how my shadow stretches across the wooden floor and climbs up the wall as I move around my room.

slides.title: Poem: My Shadow - Part 2
slides.text:
The funniest thing about him is the way he likes to grow—
Not at all like proper children, which is always very slow;
For he sometimes shoots up taller like an india-rubber ball,
And he sometimes gets so little that there's none of him at all.

I watch him in the afternoon as the sun sinks low in the west. He grows taller and taller, like a giant reaching for the tree branches. It is amazing how fast he changes his size! Normal children take many years to grow just a few inches, but my shadow can grow ten feet in a split second! Sometimes, when the sun is directly above my head at noon, he disappears completely, shrinking under my feet as if he is playing hide-and-seek. He is a magical playmate indeed, changing his shape and height constantly throughout the day to match the sun's position. Shadows change shape depending on where the light is coming from. When the light source is low, like the sun at sunrise or sunset, the shadow becomes very long. When the light is directly above, like the sun at noon, the shadow gets very short and small. This is a basic rule of science that explains why my shadow grows and shrinks.

slides.title: Poem: My Shadow - Part 3
slides.text:
One morning, very early, before the sun was up,
I rose and found the shining dew on every buttercup;
But my lazy little shadow, like an arrant sleepy-head,
Had stayed at home behind me and was fast asleep in bed.

I walked quietly through the wet grass, expecting my shadow to run along beside me as usual. But when I looked down, the ground was empty! There was no shadow to be seen anywhere. He was too lazy to wake up in the cool dawn. He stayed warm and cozy in my room, resting on the floor. I laughed to think that my shadow was a sleepy-head, missing the beautiful sunrise and the sparkling dew. This showed me that shadows need light to exist; without the sun, they must remain asleep in the darkness, waiting for the light to return and bring them back to life. In the complete darkness of the night, there is no light to block, so no shadows can be created. That is why my shadow was gone when I woke up before the sun. He needed the bright light to return so he could come out and play with me again in the garden, running beside me under the warm sun.

slides.title: Rhyme & Word Play
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Rhyming Pairs
Identify these rhyming words from the poem:
• me / see
• head / bed
• grow / slow
• ball / all

Rhymes help us remember poems and feel their musical rhythm!

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Where does the shadow jump when the child gets into bed?
2. How does the shadow's growth differ from a normal child's growth?
3. What does the shadow look like according to the poem?

slides.title: Moral Focus: Wonder & Science
slides.text:
Observing our environment with curiosity helps us learn. Shadows are formed when light is blocked by an object. By watching how they change, we explore physics!

Discuss: When is a shadow longest: at noon or in the late afternoon? Why?

wednesday.title: Phonics Fun with F and P

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 1
slides.text: Welcome to our speech gym! Let's start with a funny story about Peter and Freddy. Peter the Piper was a cheerful boy who loved pickled peppers. He decided to pack a picnic for his friends. Peter picked a peck of pickled peppers. A peck is a large basket full of fresh peppers. Peter put the peppers in a purple bag. His dog, Polly, was a pink puppy. Polly loved to play in the green park. Polly's pink puppy plays in the park all day, chasing paper airplanes and popping bubbles. Let's practice saying this fast: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers. A peck of pickled peppers Peter Piper picked. Polly's pink puppy plays in the park.' Slow down if your tongue twists, then try again! Repeat it three times to get better. This helps you speak clearly and confidently in front of others. Peter loved to share his delicious pickled peppers with all his animal friends on the farm, making sure they had enough food. Polly the puppy would run in circles, barking with pure joy as the autumn leaves fell. She liked to catch the falling leaves in her mouth and roll in the soft green grass.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 2
slides.text: Next, let's meet Freddy the Frog. Freddy was a friendly frog who lived in a fresh, cool pond. Freddy and his three frog brothers loved to swim. Four fine fresh fish fly fast in the clear stream. The fish that fly fast are four fine fresh fish. Freddy watched the fish and laughed. 'Friendly frogs float in fresh water,' said Freddy. Indeed, friendly frogs float in the fresh flow. The water was cool and clean. Let's try saying this together: 'Four fine fresh fish fly fast. The fish that fly fast are four fine fresh fish. Friendly frogs float in fresh water. Float, frogs, float in the fresh flow.' Keep your lips open slightly to make a clean /f/ sound, letting the air flow smoothly and clearly. It is a wonderful exercise for your voice. Freddy the frog also enjoyed the company of the fish, watching them swim gracefully through the clear, cool pond water. He would sit on a large green lily pad and practice his deep croaks, feeling the warm sunshine on his smooth green skin as the gentle breeze blew through the trees.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun - Part 3
slides.text: Now, let's combine the /p/ and /f/ sounds in a giant speech challenge! Peter Piper and Freddy the Frog met at the park for their big picnic. Polly the puppy ran around them. 'Pass the pink peppers, please,' quacked the duck. 'No, fish are finer for a frog,' croaked Freddy. They ate, played, and laughed. Practicing tongue twisters is like stretching your speech muscles. It trains your brain to switch between different lip and tongue positions quickly. Let's try our final super twister: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers, while four friendly frogs floated in fresh pools.' Say it three times: slowly first, then medium, then super fast! You will be a master of clear speech and pronunciation. Clear speech helps you explain your ideas to everyone. By practicing these tongue twisters every day, we train our tongues and lips to move quickly and accurately. This makes it much easier to read aloud in class and present our ideas clearly to our teachers and friends. Remember, practice makes perfect, and taking your time is the best way to learn any new skill.

slides.title: Speech & Enunciation Practice
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Contrasting /f/ and /p/
• /f/ (Friction): Place your upper teeth gently on your lower lip and blow air out (e.g., 'fish', 'frogs').
• /p/ (Plosive): Close your lips tightly, then pop them open with air (e.g., 'Peter', 'puppy').

Enunciation practice keeps our speech clear!

slides.title: Comprehension & Speech Tasks
slides.text:
Tasks:
1. Say Twister 1 and Twister 4 rapidly without mixing up the 'f' sounds.
2. Say Twister 2 and Twister 3 while focusing on clean 'p' pops.
3. Identify three words in the slides that start with 'f' and three with 'p'.

slides.title: Focus: Perseverance & Clear Speech
slides.text:
Practicing difficult sounds builds speaking confidence. It's okay to make mistakes; just slow down, breathe, and try again.

Discuss: Why is speaking clearly important when explaining a science experiment or telling a story?

slides.title: Biography: Helen Keller - Part 1
slides.text: Helen Keller was born in Alabama, USA, as a healthy baby. However, when she was just nineteen months old, she fell very sick with a high fever. This illness left her both blind and deaf. Because she could not see or hear, she had no way to speak, write, or understand the people around her. As she grew older, she felt like she was trapped in a dark, silent prison. She wanted to express her feelings, but she could not. This made her feel extremely frustrated, angry, and wild. She would throw tantrums, break dishes, and scream because she felt completely cut off from the world. Her worried family did not know how to help her learn, but they kept hoping. They looked for doctors and teachers everywhere to help their daughter. During this difficult time, Helen's parents felt very sad and worried. They did not know how to communicate with their little girl, who was often confused and scared in her dark and silent world. She wanted to tell them what she needed, but since she could not hear or see, she was locked inside her own mind.

slides.title: Biography: Helen Keller - Part 2
slides.text: Everything changed when Helen was seven years old. A patient and clever teacher named Anne Sullivan came to live with the family. Anne was determined to reach Helen's mind. She began spelling words into Helen's hand using a special sign language alphabet. For weeks, Helen copied the finger movements like a game, but she did not understand that the letters represented real things. One spring morning, Anne took Helen outside to the water pump. She held Helen's hand under the cool, running water. On Helen's other hand, Anne spelled the letters 'W-A-T-E-R' over and over. Suddenly, Helen understood! She realized that the cool flow on her hand had a name. That magic moment opened her entire world. She went inside and touched other things, wanting to learn their names. This breakthrough at the water pump was the key that unlocked Helen's mind. She realized that everything in the world had a name, and that Anne's finger movements were the secret code to learning those names. She was filled with joy and excitement, running around the yard touching flowers, trees, and stones, eager to learn their names.

slides.title: Biography: Helen Keller - Part 3
slides.text: From that day on, Helen was eager to learn everything. She touched objects and Anne spelled their names. Helen was a brilliant student. She learned to read Braille, which is a system of writing using raised dots on paper. She also learned to write using a special board, and she even learned to speak by feeling the vibrations in her teacher's throat. When she grew up, Helen went to college and became the first blind and deaf person in history to earn a college degree. She traveled all over the world, writing books and giving lectures to help other disabled people. She proved that with determination, patience, and good teachers, we can overcome any obstacle. She remains a famous symbol of hope and courage for everyone. Helen's life became a shining example of hope and perseverance for millions of people around the world. She wrote several books about her experiences and spoke in many countries, advocating for better education and support for people with disabilities. She showed the world that with patience, dedication, and love, even the greatest challenges can be overcome.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Blind - Unable to see.
• Deaf - Unable to hear.
• Palm - The inner surface of the hand.
• Braille - A system of writing for blind people using raised dots.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What illness-related challenges did Helen have as a baby?
2. What word did Helen understand when she felt the cold running water?
3. What did Helen accomplish when she grew up?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Determination and Perseverance.

No obstacle is too big to overcome when we have determination and the support of others. Hard work and patience can turn darkness into light.

Discuss: How can we help classmates who learn differently or have physical challenges?

slides.title: Fable: The Tortoise and the Hare - Part 1
slides.text: A boastful Hare lived in a green valley, and he always bragged about how fast he could run. He would tease the other animals, showing off his speed. 'I am the fastest animal in the forest!' he laughed. 'Nobody can beat me!' Tired of his constant boasting, a slow and quiet Tortoise decided to challenge him to a race. The Hare laughed out loud. 'A race against you? This is a joke!' he chuckled. The other animals gathered to watch. The start signal was given, and the Hare zoomed ahead like a rocket. Soon, he was so far ahead that he looked back and could not even see the Tortoise. Confident of his victory, the Hare decided to stop and take a nap under a shady tree. He fell asleep quickly, breathing slowly in the cool shade. The Hare was so confident of his victory that he thought he had plenty of time to rest. He walked over to a shady oak tree, lay down on the soft grass, and closed his eyes. The gentle breeze blew through the leaves, and soon the Hare was fast asleep, snoring loudly and dreaming of his certain success.

slides.title: Fable: The Tortoise and the Hare - Part 2
slides.text: While the Hare slept soundly in the cool grass, dreaming of his trophy, the Tortoise crawled slowly and steadily. The road was hot and long, and the Tortoise was very tired, but he never stopped. He kept his eyes on the finish line and moved his feet, one slow step after another. He did not look back or rest. Hours passed. The sun began to go down. The Tortoise kept walking. He passed the shady tree where the Hare was sleeping, but he did not make a sound. He walked past the sleeping Hare quietly. The Hare was snoring loudly, completely unaware that the slow Tortoise was already ahead of him, creeping closer and closer to victory. The other animals watched in quiet excitement. Step by step, the Tortoise moved forward along the dusty road under the hot sun. He did not let the heat or the long distance discourage him. He kept his mind focused on the goal and refused to stop for a rest. His slow but steady progress showed that determination is more important than speed when completing a long journey.

slides.title: Fable: The Tortoise and the Hare - Part 3
slides.text: Finally, the Tortoise was just a few feet away from the finish line. The other animals began to cheer loudly. The noise woke up the Hare. He stood up, rubbed his eyes, and looked towards the finish line. To his horror, he saw the Tortoise almost there! The Hare ran as fast as his long legs could carry him, sprinting like the wind. But it was too late. The slow Tortoise crossed the finish line just as the Hare reached it! All the animals cheered for the Tortoise. The Hare was quiet and ashamed. The Tortoise smiled and said, 'Slow and steady wins the race.' The Hare learned that boasting and laziness lead to failure, while steady work leads to success. He promised never to tease the others again. The other animals cheered and congratulated the Tortoise for his amazing victory, celebrating his hard work and perseverance. The Hare, humbled by his defeat, realized that his boastful attitude and laziness had cost him the race. From that day on, he treated everyone with respect and never bragged about his speed again.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Boastful - Showing excessive pride in oneself.
• Steadily - In a controlled, continuous, and even way.
• Crawled - Moved slowly on hands and knees or close to the ground.

Compare the speed words: 'sprint', 'run', 'walk', 'crawl', 'creep'.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Why did the Hare decide to take a nap during the race?
2. What did the Tortoise do while the Hare was sleeping?
3. Who won the race, and why?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Perseverance and Humility.

Slow and steady wins the race. Overconfidence and laziness lead to failure, while patience, steady work, and determination lead to success.

Discuss: Tell about a time you succeeded at something by not giving up, even if it took a long time.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Unggoy at ang Pagong - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Noong araw, may magkaibigang si Pagong at si Matsing. Kilala si Pagong sa pagiging mabagal ngunit napakatalino at mapagkumbaba, habang si Matsing naman ay kilala sa pagiging mabilis ngunit napakatuso at sakim. Isang araw, habang namamasyal sila sa pampang ng ilog, nakakita sila ng isang lumulutang na puno ng saging. 'Sa akin ang itaas na bahagi dahil may mga dahon na ito!' sigaw ni Matsing. Pumayag si Pagong at kinuha ang ibabang bahagi na walang dahon ngunit may mga ugat. Umuwi sila at itinanim ang kani-kanilang bahagi. Makalipas ang ilang linggo, nalanta at namatay ang itinanim ni Matsing dahil walang ugat ang kanyang saging. Samantala, ang saging ni Pagong ay lumago, naging malabay, at namunga ng marami. Nang makita ito ni Matsing, naisip niya na naman na lamangan ang kanyang mabait na kaibigan dahil alam niyang hindi kayang umakyat ni Pagong.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Unggoy at ang Pagong - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Dahil hindi makapang-akyat si Pagong, nakiusap siya kay Matsing. 'Kaibigang Matsing, maaari mo ba akong tulungang pitasin ang mga hinog na saging? Hahatiin natin ang mga bunga.' Agad na umakyat si Matsing sa puno. Ngunit sa halip na ihulog ang mga saging, kinain niya ang lahat ng masasarap at matatamis na saging sa itaas. Inihulog lamang niya ang mga balat kay Pagong habang tumatawa ng malakas. 'Napakasarap ng mga saging na ito!' biro ni Matsing habang busog na busog. Labis na nalungkot at nagalit si Pagong sa kataksilan ng kanyang kaibigan. Upang makaganti, kumuha si Pagong ng mga matutulis na tinik ng suso at ipinalibot ang mga ito sa puno ng saging. Nang bumaba si Matsing, sumigaw siya sa sakit dahil natusok ang kanyang mga kamay at paa ng matutulis na tinik.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Unggoy at ang Pagong - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Nagalit si Matsing at hinuli si Pagong. 'Ipapakain kita sa apoy o itatapon kita sa ilog!' sigaw ni Matsing. Nag-isip ng mabilis si Pagong at nagkunwaring takot sa ilog. 'Pakiusap, sunugin mo na lang ako sa apoy, huwag mo lang akong itapon sa malalim na ilog dahil malulunod ako!' pagmamakaawa ni Pagong. Akala ni Matsing ay matatalo niya si Pagong, kaya itinapon niya ito sa ilog. Ngunit laking gulat ni Matsing nang lumalangoy nang maayos si Pagong at tumatawa. 'Salamat, Matsing! Hindi mo ba alam na sa tubig ako nakatira?' sigaw ni Pagong. Natuto ng malaking leksyon si Matsing tungkol sa pagiging sakim at tuso, at mula noon ay hindi na niya niloko ang iba pang hayop sa kagubatan.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Sakim - Mapag-imbot o gustong solohin ang lahat. • Nalanta - Natuyo ang halaman at namatay. • Niloko - Dinaya o ginawan ng masama. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'ng' sa dulo ng mga salitang 'unggoy', 'pagong', at 'saging'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Anong bahagi ng puno ng saging ang pinili ni Matsing, at bakit ito namatay? 2. Paano dinaya ni Matsing si Pagong nang umakyat siya sa puno? 3. Paano nakaligtas si Pagong sa parusa ni Matsing?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang katapatan at kababaang-loob ay palaging nagtatagumpay. Ang kasakiman at panlilinlang ay nagdadala ng kapahamakan sa sarili. Mas maganda ang maging tapat sa mga kaibigan at magbahagi ng mga biyaya sa kapwa. Talakayin: Bakit mahalagang ibahagi ang mga bagay sa iyong mga kaibigan?

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Alamat ng Pinya - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Noong unang panahon, may isang mag-ina na nakatira sa isang malayong baryo. Sila ay si Aling Rosa at ang kanyang kaisa-isang anak na si Pinang. Mahal na mahal ni Aling Rosa si Pinang, ngunit lumaki si Pinang na tamad at walang ginagawa sa bahay kundi maglaro at matulog. Tuwing tuturuan siya ng kanyang ina na maglinis o magluto, palagi niyang sinasabing alam na niya ito. Ngunit kapag inutusan na siya, marami siyang idinadahilan. Isang araw, nagkasakit ng malubha si Aling Rosa at hindi makabangon sa higaan upang magluto. Nakiusap siya kay Pinang, 'Anak, pakiusap at magluto ka ng lugaw para sa ating tanghalian.' Sumang-ayon si Pinang ngunit nagreklamo pa rin habang naghahanap ng sandok sa kusina.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Alamat ng Pinya - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Dahil tamad si Pinang, hindi siya naghanap nang mabuti sa kusina. Paulit-ulit siyang nagtatanong sa kanyang maysakit na ina. 'Inay, nasaan po ang sandok?' 'Inay, nasaan po ang bigas?' 'Inay, nasaan po ang posporo?' Dahil sa matinding pagod at yamot ng ina sa paulit-ulit na tanong ni Pinang gayong malapit lang naman ang mga gamit, nasabi ni Aling Rosa: 'Naku, Pinang! Sana ay magkaroon ka ng maraming mata upang makita mo ang lahat ng bagay at hindi ka na paulit-ulit na nagtatanong sa akin!' Natahimik si Pinang at lumabas ng kusina upang maghanap muli. Lumipas ang hapon at gabi, ngunit hindi na bumalik si Pinang sa kuwarto ng ina. Nang gumaling si Aling Rosa, hinanap niya si Pinang sa buong baryo ngunit walang nakakita sa kanya.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Alamat ng Pinya - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Isang umaga, habang naglilinis si Aling Rosa sa kanilang bakuran, nakita niya ang isang kakaibang halaman na tumubo sa tabi ng kanilang kusina. Hindi pa siya nakakakita ng ganoong uri ng halaman. Mayroon itong prutas na may hugis-ulo ng tao at napapalibutan ng napakaraming parang mata sa buong katawan nito. Biglang naalala ni Aling Rosa ang kanyang huling sinabi sa anak bago ito mawala. Napaiyak siya dahil narealize niyang ang prutas na iyon ay ang kanyang anak na si Pinang na binago ng isang sumpa. Tinawag niya itong 'Pinang' bilang alaalang kanyang anak, at kalaunan ay tinawag itong 'Pinya'. Inalagaan niya ito nang mabuti at pinalaganap ang mga buto nito sa komunidad.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Baryo - Isang maliit na komunidad o nayon sa probinsya. • Yamot - Pagkainis o kawalan ng pasensya. • Sumpa - Isang mahiwagang salita na nagdudulot ng pagbabago. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'p' sa salitang 'pinya', 'pinang', at 'pagod'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Ano ang ugali ni Pinang na nagpahirap sa kanyang ina? 2. Ano ang nasabi ni Aling Rosa kay Pinang dahil sa kanyang pagkainis? 3. Ano ang kakaibang halaman na tumubo sa bakuran ni Aling Rosa?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang pagsunod sa magulang at pagkusa sa paggawa ay mahalagang katangian. Huwag maging tamad at matutong maghanap gamit ang sariling lakas bago magtanong sa iba. Ang mga salita ng magulang ay may kapangyarihan kaya dapat maging maingat sa pagsalitang iyon. Talakayin: Ano ang mga simpleng gawaing-bahay na maaari mong gawin upang makatulong sa iyong mga magulang?

math.title: Mathematics
math.subtitle: Week 1: Whole Numbers up to 10,000 & Place Value

slides.title: Deepening Place Value up to 10,000
slides.text:
At this level, we work extensively with 4-digit numbers up to 10,000.
Each place value column is 10 times larger than the column to its right (e.
g.
, 1 Ten = 10 Ones, 1 Hundred = 10 Tens, 1 Thousand = 10 Hundreds).
For instance, consider the example of Example:
10 Hundreds = 1 Thousand. 100 Tens = 1 Thousand.
Another application is demonstrated in science classrooms.
Verify these concepts by testing them with simple setups.

examples.title: Example
examples.content: 10 Hundreds = 1 Thousand. 100 Tens = 1 Thousand.

slides.title: Advanced Representations of Numbers
slides.text:
Numbers can be represented in standard digits, expanded form, word format, or Roman Numerals.
Roman numerals use letters: I = 1, V = 5, X = 10, L = 50, C = 100, D = 500, M = 1,000.
For instance, consider the example of Example:
XIV is 14 (10 + 5 - 1). XL is 40 (50 - 10).
Another application is demonstrated in science classrooms.
Verify these concepts by testing them with simple setups.
How does the concept of Advanced Representations of Numbers help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

slides.title: The Multiplier Relationship
slides.text:
Understanding that moving a digit one place value to the left multiplies its value by 10 is crucial for advanced math.
For example, the 3 in 3,000 is 10 times larger than the 3 in 300.
For instance, consider the example of Example:
Comparing digits: In 5,555, the first 5 is 10 times larger than the second 5.
Another application is demonstrated in science classrooms.
Verify these concepts by testing them with simple setups.
How does the concept of The Multiplier Relationship help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Example
examples.content: Comparing digits: In 5,555, the first 5 is 10 times larger than the second 5.

slides.title: Roman Numerals: Subtractive Rules
slides.text:
When a smaller numeral is placed before a larger numeral, subtract its value.
For example, IX means 10 - 1 = 9.
XC is 100 - 10 = 90.
For instance, consider the example of Example:
94 in Roman numerals is XCIV (90 + 4).
Another application is demonstrated in science classrooms.
Verify these concepts by testing them with simple setups.
How does the concept of Roman Numerals: Subtractive Rules help us think critically?

examples.title: Example
examples.content: 94 in Roman numerals is XCIV (90 + 4).

slides.title: Roman Numerals: Additive Rules
slides.text:
When a smaller numeral is placed after a larger numeral, add its value.
For example, LX is 50 + 10 = 60.
VIII is 5 + 3 = 8.
For instance, consider the example of Example:
158 is CLVIII.
Another application is demonstrated in science classrooms.
Verify these concepts by testing them with simple setups.
How does the concept of Roman Numerals: Additive Rules help us think critically?

examples.title: Example
examples.content: 158 is CLVIII.

Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

How many tens are equal to exactly one thousand?
What is the expanded form of 3,085?

What is the Roman numeral for 99?
Which number is 1,000 less than the largest 4-digit number?
If you multiply the Roman numeral V by the Roman numeral IX, what is the product in Roman numerals?

performance.title: Roman Numeral Watch
performance.desc: Find an image of a clock face that uses Roman numerals (or draw one yourself). Write down the Roman numerals from I to XII. Next to each, write the corresponding standard number. Show and explain your clock face and the subtractive/additive rules to your parent.
labels: Clock drawing is neat and accurate
Correct mapping of I to XII
Clear verbal explanation of rules

pages:
Exercise 1: Roman Numeral Conversions

Write the corresponding standard numbers or Roman numerals using your stylus.

1. Convert to Roman numerals: 94
XCIV
2. Convert to Roman numerals: 49
XLIX
3. Convert to standard form: XCVII
97

Exercise 2: Advanced Place Value Table

State the multiplier relationships between digit values in different columns.

Number Digit compared Digit 1 worth Digit 2 worth Relationship (How many times larger?)
3,350 3 3,000 300 10 times larger
5,050 5 5,000 50 100 times larger

Exercise 3: Roman Numeral Arithmetic

Solve the arithmetic problems and write your answer as a Roman numeral.

1. Solve: XL + LX = ?
C (40 + 60 = 100)
2. Solve: XCVIII - XIV = ?
LXXXIV (98 - 14 = 84)

science.title: Science
science.subtitle: Week 1: Science in Our Daily Life

slides.title: Advanced Scientific Inquiry
slides.text:
Scientific inquiry is a systematic way of exploring the natural world.
It involves making observations, asking testable questions, and forming hypotheses.
For instance, consider the example of Hypothesis:
An educated guess that can be tested through experiments.
Another case is the study of Systematic:
Following a step-by-step method to ensure results are reliable.
How does the concept of Advanced Scientific Inquiry help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Hypothesis
examples.content: An educated guess that can be tested through experiments.

examples.title: Systematic
examples.content: Following a step-by-step method to ensure results are reliable.

slides.title: Quantitative vs. Qualitative
slides.text:
Observations can be qualitative (describing qualities using senses) or quantitative (using numbers and measurements).
This slide introduces key insights about Quantitative vs. Qualitative.
For instance, consider the example of Qualitative:
Example: 'The chemical solution smells sour and has a bright blue color.'
Another case is the study of Quantitative:
Example: 'The plant grew 3.5 centimeters in five days.'
How does the concept of Quantitative vs. Qualitative help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Qualitative
examples.content: Example: 'The chemical solution smells sour and has a bright blue color.'

examples.title: Quantitative
examples.content: Example: 'The plant grew 3.5 centimeters in five days.'

slides.title: The Scientific Method
slides.text:
The scientific method is a tool used by scientists to study problems.
It begins with an observation and a question.
For instance, consider the example of Testable Question:
A question that can be answered by doing an experiment, not just looking up facts.
Another case is the study of Variables:
Factors in an experiment that can change, like temperature or water amount.
How does the concept of The Scientific Method help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Testable Question
examples.content: A question that can be answered by doing an experiment, not just looking up facts.

examples.title: Variables
examples.content: Factors in an experiment that can change, like temperature or water amount.

slides.title: Atmospheric Science: Weather
slides.text:
Weather is driven by temperature, air pressure, and moisture in the atmosphere.
Science helps us forecast these shifts.
For instance, consider the example of Air Temperature:
Determines whether moisture falls as rain, snow, or hail.
Another case is the study of Evaporation Cycle:
Solar energy turns surface water into vapor, fueling cloud formation.
How does the concept of Atmospheric Science: Weather help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Air Temperature
examples.content: Determines whether moisture falls as rain, snow, or hail.

examples.title: Evaporation Cycle
examples.content: Solar energy turns surface water into vapor, fueling cloud formation.

slides.title: Botanical Adaptations
slides.text:
Plants have specialized structures that help them survive in their local environment.
This slide introduces key insights about Botanical Adaptations.
For instance, consider the example of Photosynthesis:
The process by which plants use sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water to create chemical energy.
Another case is the study of Tropism:
Growth response towards an external stimulus, such as phototropism (light).
How does the concept of Botanical Adaptations help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Photosynthesis
examples.content: The process by which plants use sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water to create chemical energy.

examples.title: Tropism
examples.content: Growth response towards an external stimulus, such as phototropism (light).

slides.title: Zoological Classification
slides.text:
Animals are classified by their physical structures, diets, and habitats.
This slide introduces key insights about Zoological Classification.
For instance, consider the example of Vertebrates:
Animals with backbones, like mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and fish.
Another case is the study of Invertebrates:
Animals without backbones, like insects, spiders, and crabs.
How does the concept of Zoological Classification help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Vertebrates
examples.content: Animals with backbones, like mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and fish.

examples.title: Invertebrates
examples.content: Animals without backbones, like insects, spiders, and crabs.

slides.title: Thermodynamics: Heat Transfer
slides.text:
Heat transfers from hotter objects to colder ones until they reach the same temperature.
This slide introduces key insights about Thermodynamics: Heat Transfer.
For instance, consider the example of Conduction:
Heat transfer through direct touch, like a spoon heating up in hot soup.
Another case is the study of Convection:
Heat transfer in liquids and gases as warm parts rise and cool parts sink.
How does the concept of Thermodynamics: Heat Transfer help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Conduction
examples.content: Heat transfer through direct touch, like a spoon heating up in hot soup.

examples.title: Convection
examples.content: Heat transfer in liquids and gases as warm parts rise and cool parts sink.

slides.title: Optics: Light Refraction
slides.text:
Light bends when it passes from one medium to another, such as from air into water.
This slide introduces key insights about Optics: Light Refraction.
For instance, consider the example of Refraction:
Bending of light waves which makes straws in water look broken.
Another case is the study of Lenses:
Curved glass pieces that bend light to magnify objects.
How does the concept of Optics: Light Refraction help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Refraction
examples.content: Bending of light waves which makes straws in water look broken.

examples.title: Lenses
examples.content: Curved glass pieces that bend light to magnify objects.

slides.title: Physics: Force and Friction
slides.text:
Friction is a force that opposes motion when two surfaces rub against each other.
This slide introduces key insights about Physics: Force and Friction.
For instance, consider the example of Friction:
Rough surfaces create more friction, slowing moving objects down.
Another case is the study of Lubricants:
Oil or water reduces friction, allowing parts to slide smoothly.
How does the concept of Physics: Force and Friction help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Friction
examples.content: Rough surfaces create more friction, slowing moving objects down.

examples.title: Lubricants
examples.content: Oil or water reduces friction, allowing parts to slide smoothly.

slides.title: Phase Changes of Matter
slides.text:
Adding or removing heat energy causes matter to change its physical state.
This slide introduces key insights about Phase Changes of Matter.
For instance, consider the example of Sublimation:
Solid turning directly into gas, like dry ice evaporating.
Another case is the study of Condensation:
Gas cooling down to turn into a liquid, like dew on grass.
How does the concept of Phase Changes of Matter help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Sublimation
examples.content: Solid turning directly into gas, like dry ice evaporating.

examples.title: Condensation
examples.content: Gas cooling down to turn into a liquid, like dew on grass.

slides.title: Meteorology: Clouds & Rain
slides.text:
Clouds are made of billions of tiny water droplets that condense around dust particles in the air.
This slide introduces key insights about Meteorology: Clouds & Rain.
For instance, consider the example of Precipitation:
When droplets grow too heavy, gravity pulls them down as rain.
Another case is the study of Cloud Types:
Cumulus (fluffy), Stratus (flat layers), and Cirrus (wispy high clouds).
How does the concept of Meteorology: Clouds & Rain help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Precipitation
examples.content: When droplets grow too heavy, gravity pulls them down as rain.

examples.title: Cloud Types
examples.content: Cumulus (fluffy), Stratus (flat layers), and Cirrus (wispy high clouds).

slides.title: Aerodynamics: Air Resistance
slides.text:
Air resistance is a type of friction that slows down objects moving through the air.
This slide introduces key insights about Aerodynamics: Air Resistance.
For instance, consider the example of Drag:
Flat objects experience more drag because they push more air.
Another case is the study of Streamlining:
Shapes that cut through air easily, like airplanes or birds.
How does the concept of Aerodynamics: Air Resistance help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Drag
examples.content: Flat objects experience more drag because they push more air.

examples.title: Streamlining
examples.content: Shapes that cut through air easily, like airplanes or birds.

slides.title: Pedology: Soil Composition
slides.text:
Soil is a mixture of weathered rock, organic matter, water, and air.
This slide introduces key insights about Pedology: Soil Composition.
For instance, consider the example of Humus:
Decayed plant and animal matter that provides nutrients for plants.
Another case is the study of Soil Horizons:
Layers of soil: topsoil (rich in humus), subsoil, and bedrock.
How does the concept of Pedology: Soil Composition help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Humus
examples.content: Decayed plant and animal matter that provides nutrients for plants.

examples.title: Soil Horizons
examples.content: Layers of soil: topsoil (rich in humus), subsoil, and bedrock.

slides.title: Astronomy: Earth's Rotation
slides.text:
The Earth rotates counter-clockwise on its axis once every 24 hours.
This slide introduces key insights about Astronomy: Earth's Rotation.
For instance, consider the example of Apparent Motion:
The Sun appears to move across the sky because of Earth's spin.
Another case is the study of Time Zones:
Different parts of Earth experience daylight at different times.
How does the concept of Astronomy: Earth's Rotation help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Apparent Motion
examples.content: The Sun appears to move across the sky because of Earth's spin.

examples.title: Time Zones
examples.content: Different parts of Earth experience daylight at different times.

slides.title: Chemistry: Oxidation
slides.text:
Rusting is a chemical reaction called oxidation, where iron combines with oxygen and moisture.
This slide introduces key insights about Chemistry: Oxidation.
For instance, consider the example of Iron Oxide:
The chemical name for rust, which ruins the strength of metal.
Another case is the study of Galvanization:
Coating iron with zinc to prevent oxygen from reaching the metal.
How does the concept of Chemistry: Oxidation help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Iron Oxide
examples.content: The chemical name for rust, which ruins the strength of metal.

examples.title: Galvanization
examples.content: Coating iron with zinc to prevent oxygen from reaching the metal.

slides.title: Microbiology: Pathogens
slides.text:
Germs are microscopic pathogens (bacteria, viruses, fungi) that can cause infections.
This slide introduces key insights about Microbiology: Pathogens.
For instance, consider the example of Antiseptic:
Chemicals that kill or prevent the growth of microorganisms.
Another case is the study of Immune System:
The body's natural defense network that fights off pathogens.
How does the concept of Microbiology: Pathogens help us think critically?
Keep up your curiosity and check your facts as a scientist!

examples.title: Antiseptic
examples.content: Chemicals that kill or prevent the growth of microorganisms.

examples.title: Immune System
examples.content: The body's natural defense network that fights off pathogens.

slides.title: Biochemistry: Cellular Energy
slides.text:
Cells break down glucose molecules to release energy in a process called respiration.
This slide introduces key insights about Biochemistry: Cellular Energy.
For instance, consider the example of Glucose:
A simple sugar that serves as the primary energy source for cells.

All chemical reactions in a living organism that sustain life.

slides.title: Welcome to the Language Safari!
slides.text:
Hello, Explorer!
Welcome to English Week 1!
This week, we are going on an exciting language safari.
We will explore our neighborhood to spot word patterns.
We will learn about words that end in double consonants.
We will also learn how to point to things using pronouns.
Finally, we will read stories about local adventures.
Let's keep our eyes open and observe closely.

slides.title: Spotting Ending Sounds (CVCC)
slides.text:
Look at your hand.
Let's say it slowly: h-a-n-d.
Hear how the letters 'n' and 'd' blend at the end?
This is a CVCC word: Consonant-Vowel-Consonant-Consonant.
It ends with two consonants that make a blended sound.
We call these ending consonant blends in phonics.
They are very common in the words we use every day.
Let's learn how to spot and spell them correctly.

examples.title: Word Pattern
examples.content: C = Consonant, V = Vowel, C = Consonant, C = Consonant.

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: In the Tent
slides.text:
Imagine we are camping under the dark night sky.
We set up a strong green tent on the ground.
Look at the word 'tent'.
It ends with 'nt'.
The letters 'n' and 't' blend together smoothly.
Let's practice saying the word 'tent' three times.
Feel the blend of 'n' and 't' on your tongue.
We spell it as t - e - n - t.

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Under the Lamp
slides.text:
When the sun sets, the room becomes very dark.
We turn on the brass lamp sitting on our desk.
Look at the word 'lamp'.
It ends with 'mp'.
The letters 'm' and 'p' blend at the very end.
What does a lamp help us observe at night?
It shines a bright light on our notebook pages.
We spell it as l - a - m - p.

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: The Pink Flower
slides.text:
Let's take a walk in the green garden.
We observe a beautiful pink flower opening its petals.
Look at the word 'pink'.
It ends with 'nk'.
The letters 'n' and 'k' form a blended sound.
How many pink things can you see in your room?
Maybe a pink toy, a pink pen, or a pink shirt.
We spell it as p - i - n - k.

slides.title: Phonics Hunt: Keeping Active
slides.text:
To keep our bodies strong, we must stay active.
We bend our knees before we run in the park.
We wear a tight leather belt to hold our shorts.
Look at 'bend' (ends in 'nd') and 'belt' (ends in 'lt').
Both of these words are CVCC spelling patterns.
They show how consonants pair up at the end.
We spell them as b-e-n-d and b-e-l-t.
Practice writing these action words on your paper!

Listen to the wind blowing through the bamboo trees.

After running in the wind, we feel very thirsty.
We drink a cold glass of milk to get energy.
The word 'milk' ends with the blend 'lk'.
Both 'wind' and 'milk' are common CVCC words.

Both 'desk' and 'hunt' help us talk about play.

slides.title: Grammar: Pointing to Things
slides.text:
When we observe nature, we need to point to things.
We use special words called demonstrative pronouns.
These pronouns tell us if an object is near or far.
They also tell us if there is one object or many.
The four pronouns are: this, that, these, and those.
They help make our descriptions clear and specific.
Let's learn how to use them like real scientists.
We will practice pointing to objects in our room!

examples.title: Focus Pronouns
examples.content: This, That, These, Those

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Near Us: This and These
slides.text:
Look at the pencil you are holding in your hand.
You can say: 'This is my pencil.' (one object).
Now look at the fingers on your own hand.
You can say: 'These are my fingers.' (many objects).
We use 'this' for a single object near us.
We use 'these' for multiple objects close to us.
Both words show that the objects are within reach.
Try touching a book and saying: 'This is my book.'.

examples.title: Near Examples
examples.content: This pencil (1 near), These rocks (many near).

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Far From Us: That and Those
slides.text:
Look out the window at the distant sky.
You see a bird and say: 'That is a bird.' (one far).
You see clouds and say: 'Those are clouds.' (many far).
We use 'that' for a single object far away.
We use 'those' for multiple objects far away.
These words show that the objects are out of reach.
Point to a distant tree and say: 'That is a tree.' Point to stars and say: 'Those are stars.'.
For instance, consider the example of Far Examples:

examples.content: That star (1 far), Those mountains (many far).

slides.title: Sentence Engine: Declarative Sentences
slides.text:
A telling sentence is like a small train.
It makes a statement to tell a fact or observation.
We call this a declarative sentence in grammar.
It must always start with a capital letter.
It must always end with a period punctuation mark.
It has a subject (who or what the sentence is about).
It has a predicate (what the subject is or does).
Let's build a declarative sentence together!

examples.title: Rule
examples.content: Always start with a capital letter and end with a period.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Declarative: Spotting the Parts
slides.text:
Let's look at: 'Matteo rides a calesa.' 'Matteo' is the subject because he is the person.
'rides a calesa' is the predicate (the action).
Together, they tell us a complete factual statement.
Notice the capital 'M' at the beginning of the sentence.
Notice the period at the end of the sentence.
Without a subject and predicate, a sentence is incomplete.
Always check both parts when you write in your journal!
For instance, consider the example of Subject:

examples.title: Subject
examples.content: Matteo

examples.title: Predicate
examples.content: rides a calesa

slides.title: Story Time: The Calesa Ride
slides.text:
Let's read a short story about Matteo's day: 'Matteo sits inside a wooden calesa on the street.
The fast horse walks down the paved town road.
Matteo smiles at the colorful houses along the way.
He observes the busy community starting their morning.' This story is made of declarative sentences.
They tell us exactly what Matteo does and sees.
Read it aloud to practice your reading flow!
For instance, consider the example of Reading Task:
Read the story aloud to practice your spelling words!

slides.title: Characters: Who is in the Story?
slides.text:
Every story has characters that make it interesting.
Characters are the people, animals, or creatures.
They perform actions and speak words in the plot.
In our story, the characters are Matteo and the horse.
Understanding characters helps us connect with the story.
We can think about how they feel and what they want.
Who is your favorite character in the calesa story?
Write down their names on your paper!

examples.content: Characters = Who

slides.title: Setting: Where and When?
slides.text:
The setting is where and when the story takes place.
It provides the background environment for the characters.
In our story, the setting is a historic town street.
The time of the story is a sunny morning.
Knowing the setting helps us picture the events.
It is like looking at a photograph of the scene.
Where does your favorite daily activity take place?
That place is the setting of your own daily story!

slides.title: Plot: What Happens?
slides.text:
The plot is the sequence of events in a story.
It tells us what happens first, next, and last.
First, Matteo gets inside the wooden calesa.
Next, the horse walks down the paved town street.
Finally, Matteo smiles at the colorful houses.
A clear plot makes stories easy to understand.
It helps us follow the character's journey.
Can you retell the plot of Matteo's ride in order?

slides.title: Observing Local Transport
slides.text:
In our local community, we observe unique transport.
A calesa is a carriage drawn by a horse on land.
It has two large wheels and a wooden passenger cabin.
A banca is a small outrigger boat used on water.
It helps fishermen travel across rivers and seas.
Both transport types are important to our heritage.
They are perfect topics for our declarative sentences.
Let's write a sentence about a banca today!

slides.title: Nature Connections: The Wet Sand
slides.text:
Let's walk down to the riverbank to explore.
We observe wet sand near the flowing water.
Let's write: 'This sand is wet and cold.' 'Sand' is a CVCC spelling word ending in 'nd'.
'This' points to the sand right under our feet.
This sentence is a telling (declarative) sentence.
It starts with a capital letter and ends with a period.
We are using English to describe our science observations!

slides.title: Science Connection: The Pink Fish
slides.text:
Look at the clear water in the stream.
We point to a swimming fish: 'That is a pink fish.' 'Pink' is a CVCC word ending in the blend 'nk'.
'That' points to the fish swimming far from us.
This is a factual observation about the natural world.
It uses our senses to collect details about animals.
Always look closely at the color of fish scales.
What other scale colors can you observe in the water?

slides.text:
Let's play a game to spot CVCC words.
Read this: 'The child sat at the wooden desk.' Which word ends with a double consonant blend?
Yes, the word is 'desk'!
It ends with 'sk'.
The other words do not follow the CVCC pattern.
Spelling 'desk' correctly requires the letters d-e-s-k.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Near or Far?
slides.text:
Let's practice choosing the correct pronoun.
If you are holding a flower, what do you say?
'A.
This is a flower.' or 'B.
That is a flower.'?
Since the flower is in your hand, choose A!
If a bird is high on a tree branch, you say: 'That is a bird on the branch.' Use 'this' for near, and 'that' for far away.
It is as simple as measuring distance with your eyes!

examples.title: Rule of Thumb
examples.content: If you can touch it, use 'this' or 'these'.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Capital and Period
slides.text:
Let's check the rules for telling sentences.
Look at: 'the banca floats in the river' What is missing from this declarative sentence?
It must start with a capital 'T' for 'The'.
It must end with a period at the end of 'river'.
Let's write it correctly: 'The banca floats in the river.' Now it has a subject and a predicate.
It is a complete and correct sentence!
For instance, consider the example of Corrected:
'The banca floats in the river.'

slides.title: Common Error: Mixing Near and Far
slides.text:
A common mistake is using 'this' for far objects.
If a star is shining in the sky, do not say: 'This is a bright star.' (It is too far to touch!).
Instead, say: 'That is a bright star.' If the books are far on the shelf, do not say: 'These are my books.' (They are out of reach!).
Instead, say: 'Those are my books.' Always check the distance before you choose the pronoun!
For instance, consider the example of Correction:
'Those clouds look soft.'

slides.title: Summary of Week 1
slides.text:
Congratulations!
You completed all the slides for Week 1.

We learned to decode CVCC words like tent, lamp, and desk.
We learned to point using this, that, these, and those.
We learned to write declarative sentences with periods.
We learned to identify story characters, settings, and plots.
We connected our English lessons to science observations.
